candidate_problems.text: How can the conjecture be made operational enough to distinguish when interpreter systems are the primary causal drivers of concentration rather than downstream reflections of underlying product superiority, economies of scale, or consumer preference? The unresolved tension is causal identification: the claim is strong only if layered interpreters have independent explanatory and predictive power across cases, with clear criteria for what counts as an interpreter layer and how to measure its amplification of local advantage versus regeneration of rivalry.

What determines the threshold or phase transition at which beneficial coordination mechanisms—standards, platforms, financing concentration, data feedback, defaults—stop improving market efficiency and start canalizing markets into winner-take-all dominance? The open question is whether there is a general structural metric for 'contestability regeneration' versus 'advantage amplification' that predicts when the same institutions shift from productive coordination to hereditary lock-in.

Can interpreter systems be redesigned to preserve innovation and coordination benefits without recreating dominance through new layers of control, or does intervention simply relocate interpretive power elsewhere (for example from firms to regulators, standards bodies, or infrastructure providers)? This is a major unresolved design problem because the conjecture identifies leverage in restructuring interpreters, but leaves open the governance architecture that avoids both incumbent entrenchment and over-centralized counter-control.
